agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics system with an established Shipment-Carrier assignment model is being asked to support automatic splitting of heavy shipments across multiple carriers. This directly conflicts with two foundational invariants of the existing domain model.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of cargo to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for physically transporting a Shipment."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The locked binding between a Shipment and its single Carrier. Once set, only a Supervisor can change it."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the sole authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-Shipment","definition":"(Proposed, not yet in ubiquitous language) A child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment that exceeds a weight threshold."},{"term":"Auto-Split","definition":"(Proposed, not yet in ubiquitous language) An automated process that decomposes a Shipment exceeding 500 kg into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different Carrier without Supervisor approval."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is released for transport; the system guarantees exactly one Carrier is assigned at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency and state integrity constraint. A Shipment with zero or multiple Carriers creates ambiguous responsibility for cargo — the system cannot determine who is liable, who to dispatch, or who to pay. If violated, the system is in a corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"For any Shipment in any state, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization integrity constraint. Carrier assignment is a locked binding that protects against unauthorized rerouting of cargo, which has direct liability, insurance, and chain-of-custody implications. Bypassing this corrupts the authorization model.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to change Carrier assignment without Supervisor authorization must be rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment has exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a physical constraint — cargo cannot be simultaneously transported by multiple carriers at the moment of dispatch. It also ensures clear operational accountability.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must fail if the Shipment does not have exactly one assigned Carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A shipment of exactly 500 kg — does it split or not?","expected_behavior":"Cannot define without resolving the contradiction. The threshold boundary condition (<=500 vs <500) is secondary to the fundamental model conflict."},{"scenario":"A shipment is split into sub-shipments, and one sub-shipment's carrier becomes unavailable.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define. Under current rules, reassignment requires a Supervisor. The auto-split feature explicitly bypasses Supervisor involvement, creating an unresolvable conflict."},{"scenario":"A parent shipment is split, then a customer requests cancellation of the original shipment.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define. The domain has no concept of parent-child shipment relationships. Cancellation semantics are undefined for split shipments."},{"scenario":"A shipment weighing 1500 kg is split into 3 sub-shipments, and one sub-shipment fails delivery.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define. Liability, SLA, and delivery-complete semantics are undefined when a single logical shipment spans multiple carriers."},{"scenario":"Carrier assignment happens before weight is finalized (e.g., cargo is weighed at pickup).","expected_behavior":"Cannot define. If the split happens after initial carrier assignment, it would require reassigning or removing the original carrier — which requires Supervisor authority."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. Splitting shipments across carriers changes billing, insurance liability, and SLA penalty calculations. None of these financial models have been adapted for multi-carrier shipments.","operational":"Severe. The auto-split feature introduces a parent-child shipment concept that does not exist in the current domain model. Every downstream process (tracking, proof-of-delivery, exception handling, invoicing) assumes one Shipment = one Carrier.","user":"Customer-facing tracking and communication would need to handle the concept of a shipment being fulfilled by multiple carriers. Customers expect a single tracking number and a single point of accountability.","risk":"Critical. Bypassing Supervisor authorization for carrier assignment undermines the chain-of-custody guarantee. In regulated logistics (hazmat, pharmaceuticals, high-value goods), this could create compliance violations.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must handle a new shipment topology (parent + children) that does not exist today. Dispatch workflows assume 1:1 Shipment:Carrier."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their authority over carrier assignment is being bypassed. This changes their operational role and accountability model."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"May receive partial loads from split shipments. Carrier contracts, rate cards, and SLAs likely assume whole-shipment responsibility."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Lose single-carrier accountability. Delivery coordination becomes more complex. Dispute resolution is unclear when multiple carriers are involved."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Invoicing, cost allocation, and insurance models assume one carrier per shipment. Splitting requires new billing logic."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Chain-of-custody documentation, liability assignment, and regulatory reporting assume one carrier per shipment."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction between the proposed feature and two established production invariants (one-carrier-per-shipment and supervisor-only reassignment). Until the product team clarifies whether they intend to (a) redefine what a Shipment is, (b) introduce a new Sub-Shipment aggregate, or (c) relax the existing invariants, no acceptance criteria can be confidently defined. The contradiction is not a matter of interpretation — it is structural.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between auto-split and the one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant is unambiguous","The bypass of Supervisor authority is a clear violation of BR-2","The 500 kg threshold itself is a policy (invariant: false) and is not the source of the conflict"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends Sub-Shipment to be a new aggregate root or a child entity","Whether the existing invariants are open to renegotiation or must be preserved","How downstream processes (billing, tracking, compliance) should adapt"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model as described is accurate and in production","No prior concept of parent-child shipment relationships exists in the system","Supervisor authority over carrier reassignment is an intentional business control, not an implementation artifact"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly contradicts BR-1 (one Carrier per Shipment) and BR-2 (Supervisor-only reassignment). A shipment split across multiple carriers either (a) violates the one-carrier invariant on the original shipment, or (b) requires creating new sub-shipment entities with independent carrier assignments — which bypasses Supervisor authority. These are not edge cases; they are fundamental conflicts with the production domain model.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Supervisors","Carriers","Customers","Finance","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["Option A: Introduce a new 'Sub-Shipment' aggregate that is a first-class domain concept. The parent Shipment becomes a logical grouping. Each Sub-Shipment independently satisfies BR-1 (exactly one Carrier). Auto-split becomes a domain event that creates Sub-Shipments. Supervisor authority applies to Sub-Shipment reassignment, not initial automated creation.","Option B: Relax BR-1 to allow multi-carrier shipments above a weight threshold. This is a fundamental model change that affects every downstream system.","Option C: Keep the auto-split but require Supervisor approval for the split operation itself (preserving BR-2). The system proposes the split; a Supervisor confirms it."],"recommendation":"Option A. It resolves the contradiction without weakening existing invariants. Sub-Shipment becomes a new bounded concept with its own lifecycle, and the parent Shipment's one-carrier invariant is preserved by redefining it as a logical container. However, this requires significant domain modeling work and stakeholder alignment before any implementation begins. Option C is the lowest-risk interim solution if the feature is urgently needed."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The auto-split feature assigns multiple carriers to what is conceptually a single shipment, violating the one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"The auto-split feature bypasses Supervisor authority by automatically assigning carriers to newly created sub-shipments without Supervisor involvement.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"}]}
